ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cookie class; no specific entry for deep-fried butterfly cookie. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKN covers biscuits and cookies broadly; no specific code for deep-fried butterfly cookie variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for butterfly cookie; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of butterfly cookie is one piece weighing approximately 20 g, providing around 94 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Butterfly Cookie — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Butterfly Cookie (CR ID F11080007). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from palm oil fatty acid composition as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wheat-Based Biscuits and Cookies — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for butterfly cookie in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to plain wheat-flour biscuits and cookies (GI range 50–60) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) and is classified as low per the standard University of Sydney GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 11.76) / 100 ≈ 6.5, based on a 20 g serving containing 11.76 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 941 mg/100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid butterfly cookies entirely due to extremely high sodium content (941 mg/100 g), which can acutely elevate blood pressure and undermine sodium restriction targets of less than 2000 mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (58.8 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) and added sugar (10.3 g/100 g), producing a significant glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid butterfly cookies because the high refined carbohydrate content (58.8 g/100 g), low fibre (2.0 g/100 g), and added sugar (10.3 g/100 g) combine to produce rapid postprandial glucose elevation that is difficult to manage within daily carbohydrate budgets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (23.1 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g from palm oil frying, combined with very high sodium (941 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid butterfly cookies due to the combination of high saturated fat from palm oil deep-frying (~8.5 g/100 g), very high sodium (941 mg/100 g), and high caloric density (472 kcal/100 g), all of which adversely affect lipid profiles, blood pressure, and overall cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butterfly cookie (蝴蝶饼, Biskut Rama-Rama) is a traditional deep-fried snack popular in Singapore and among Chinese communities in Southeast Asia, particularly associated with Chinese New Year celebrations. The cookie is made from a simple dough of wheat flour, eggs, sugar, and palm oil, which is shaped into a distinctive butterfly form and deep-fried in palm oil until golden and crisp. The result is a light, crunchy, slightly sweet biscuit with a characteristic airy, layered texture achieved through the frying process. Nutritionally, butterfly cookie is energy-dense at 472 kcal per 100 g, with 23.1 g fat (predominantly from palm oil), 58.8 g carbohydrate, 7.1 g protein, and notably high sodium at 941 mg per 100 g. Dietary fibre is low at 2.0 g per 100 g. The high sodium content is clinically significant and makes this snack unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or fluid retention. The refined carbohydrate profile with low fibre and GI of 55 (low category, upper boundary) makes it unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to high total carbohydrate load. The high saturated fat from palm oil frying raises cardiovascular concern. A typical serving is one piece (~20 g), providing approximately 94 kcal, 4.6 g fat, and 11.8 g carbohydrate. This snack is best consumed only occasionally and in very small quantities by healthy individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a butterfly cookie?
Is butterfly cookie suitable for hypertension?
What are the ingredients in butterfly cookie?
Is butterfly cookie a Chinese New Year snack?
What is the sodium content of butterfly cookie?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080007) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (medium confidence for GI component only). Saturated fat estimated from palm oil composition literature as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for butterfly cookie are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11080007) as of 2026-04-16. These fields were also not available in USDA FoodData Central for a directly equivalent deep-fried wheat-flour butterfly cookie product. 2. Saturated fat estimated at ~8.5 g/100 g based on palm oil fatty acid composition (approximately 50% saturated fat of total fat 23.1 g/100 g); this is an approximation as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. 3. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using plain wheat-flour biscuits/cookies as the reference food; no direct GI measurement for butterfly cookie was found. GI 55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney standard scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); gi_category is set to 'low' accordingly. 4. The input record listed 'rheumatoid_arthritis' health suitability with claims of trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g and saturated fat ~10 g/100 g; these specific values are not confirmed by HPB source data and have been replaced with HPB-grounded estimates. Trans fat is set to -1 as HPB does not report this value. 5. Halal status assumed as ingredients (wheat flour, eggs, sugar, palm oil) are halal-compatible; formal halal certification status not confirmed.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low': GI value 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category per the University of Sydney standard classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Previous iteration incorrectly assigned 'medium'.

Expanded notes field to explicitly document all -1 sentinel values with reason (not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database F11080007 as of 2026-04-16) and source consulted (HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central), satisfying G3 numeric hygiene requirement.
Updated gi_source.source_match_notes to clarify GI 55 classification as low per University of Sydney scale.
Updated llm_training.natural_language_description to reflect corrected gi_category of 'low'.
